title: Microsoft Defender for Cloud
meta: 6 modules · 48 units · 4 hr 17 min · Cloud security posture, multicloud onboarding, workload protection, alerts, and response

focusAreas.title: Cloud posture and coverage
focusAreas.description: Connect Azure and hybrid resources, extend multicloud coverage, and manage cloud security posture.

focusAreas.title: Workload threat response
focusAreas.description: Enable workload protections, investigate alerts, and remediate risks across cloud resources.

modules.title: Plan for cloud workload protections using Microsoft Defender for Cloud
modules.focus: Focus: Defender for Cloud architecture, CSPM, cloud workload protection, the protection plans, and initial enablement.

units.title: Introduction
units.bodyHtml:
Microsoft Defender for Cloud protects Azure, hybrid, and multicloud environments through security posture management and workload threat protection. This module establishes the service model and the choices required before deployment.

Official moduleObjectives and prerequisites

units.title: Explain Microsoft Defender for Cloud
units.bodyHtml:
Defender for Cloud has two complementary pillars. Cloud security posture management (CSPM) discovers weaknesses, measures secure score, and recommends hardening. Cloud workload protection (CWP) detects and responds to threats against specific resource types.

1 · AssessContinuously inventory resources and configurations.
2 · SecurePrioritize recommendations and improve secure score.
3 · DefendDetect attacks and produce contextual alerts.
4 · RespondInvestigate, remediate, and automate actions.
Exam takeaway: posture management reduces the probability of compromise; workload protection detects active threats.
Official lessonCSPM and CWP overview

units.title: Describe Microsoft Defender for Cloud workload protections
units.bodyHtml:
Protection is licensed through workload-specific Defender plans. Plans cover Servers, App Service, Storage, Databases, Containers, Key Vault, Resource Manager, and APIs. Azure Arc extends supported protections to servers and Kubernetes clusters outside Azure.

  • Prevent: secure score, recommendations, regulatory compliance, just-in-time access, and adaptive controls.
  • Detect: behavioral analytics, threat intelligence, anomaly detection, and service-native telemetry.
  • Integrate: export alerts to Microsoft Sentinel or another SIEM/SOAR and use Microsoft Defender XDR integrations.
Official lessonWorkload protection plans and capabilities

units.title: Exercise - Microsoft Defender for Cloud interactive guide
units.bodyHtml:
Use the interactive guide to recognize the portal areas for Overview, Recommendations, Inventory, Regulatory compliance, Workload protections, Security alerts, and Environment settings. Practice mapping each area to assess, secure, defend, or respond.

Official interactive exerciseGuided Defender for Cloud portal tour

units.title: Enable Microsoft Defender for Cloud
units.bodyHtml:
Open Environment settings, select the management group, subscription, or connected cloud account, and enable all plans or only the required workload plans. Owner, Contributor, or Security Admin permissions are commonly required for configuration.

  1. Define the environment and regulatory scope.
  2. Select CSPM and workload plans according to risk and licensing.
  3. Configure monitoring components and data collection.
  4. Verify coverage, recommendations, and alerts after onboarding.
Exam takeaway: enabling Defender for Cloud is not a single global switch; protection plans and monitoring settings are configured per environment.
Official lessonEnable plans and monitoring

units.title: Module assessment
units.bodyHtml:
Review the distinction between CSPM and CWP, the assess-secure-defend lifecycle, workload-specific plans, Azure Arc coverage, and Environment settings.

Official assessmentPlanning knowledge check

units.title: Summary and resources
units.bodyHtml:
Plan Defender for Cloud by identifying environments and workloads, measuring posture, choosing the required Defender plans, and deciding how alerts flow into security operations.

Official moduleSummary and resources

modules.title: Connect Azure assets to Microsoft Defender for Cloud
modules.focus: Focus: asset inventory, automatic and manual provisioning, Azure Monitor Agent, and Data Collection Rules.

units.title: Introduction
units.bodyHtml:
Connected resources must be visible and supply the required telemetry before posture and workload protections can operate. This module covers inventory and the provisioning methods for Azure assets.

Official moduleObjectives and prerequisites

units.title: Explore and manage your resources with asset inventory
units.bodyHtml:
Asset inventory provides one searchable view of resources connected to Defender for Cloud. Filter by subscription, resource type, security state, recommendation, tag, or environment; inspect unhealthy and unmonitored resources; export CSV; or open the underlying Azure Resource Graph query.

  • Apply tags or launch Logic Apps for selected resources.
  • Onboard unmonitored servers where supported.
  • Use resource details to review recommendations and security findings.
Official lessonInventory, filtering, and bulk actions

units.title: Configure auto provisioning
units.bodyHtml:
Auto provisioning deploys required monitoring components consistently across eligible resources. Defender for Cloud uses Azure Policy DeployIfNotExists effects and the environment's Settings & monitoring configuration.

The Azure Monitor Agent (AMA) is the recommended collection agent. Defender for Endpoint integration can automatically onboard supported servers when Defender for Servers is enabled. The legacy Log Analytics agent reached retirement in August 2024.

Exam takeaway: prefer centralized automatic provisioning; use manual onboarding only for controlled exceptions.
Official lessonPolicy-based agent provisioning

units.title: Manual agent provisioning
units.bodyHtml:
Manual AMA deployment uses a Data Collection Rule (DCR) to define which resources collect which data and where it is sent.

  1. Create the DCR and choose platform and region.
  2. Add target resources.
  3. Add data sources such as Windows events, performance counters, Syslog, text, or IIS logs.
  4. Select a Log Analytics workspace destination.
  5. Verify agent health and heartbeat records.

Avoid attaching overlapping DCRs that collect the same stream twice. Direct MDE onboarding can protect a non-Azure server, but it does not provide Azure Arc management capabilities.

Official lessonAMA and Data Collection Rules

units.title: Module assessment
units.bodyHtml:
Distinguish asset inventory from Azure Resource Graph, automatic from manual provisioning, AMA from the retired legacy agent, and a DCR's source-resource-destination model.

Official assessmentAzure connection knowledge check

units.title: Summary and resources
units.bodyHtml:
Use inventory to find coverage gaps, automatic provisioning for consistent deployment, and carefully scoped DCRs when manual AMA configuration is necessary.

Official moduleSummary and resources

modules.title: Connect non-Azure resources to Microsoft Defender for Cloud
modules.focus: Focus: Azure Arc, non-Azure servers, AWS accounts, GCP projects, and multicloud permissions.

units.title: Introduction
units.bodyHtml:
Defender for Cloud provides one security view across Azure, on-premises, AWS, and Google Cloud. Each connection method must establish inventory, permissions, and the monitoring components needed by selected plans.

Official moduleMulticloud objectives

units.title: Protect non-Azure resources
units.bodyHtml:
Azure Arc projects servers and Kubernetes clusters from other clouds or on-premises into the Azure control plane. Arc enables Azure resource identity, policy, extensions, tagging, inventory, and supported Defender for Cloud protections.

Exam takeaway: Arc-enabled is more than security-agent onboarding; it makes the external machine an Azure-manageable resource.
Official lessonAzure Arc and hybrid protection

units.title: Connect non-Azure machines
units.bodyHtml:
Onboard a machine to Azure Arc using an installation script, supported configuration tooling, or at scale through service-principal-based deployment. Then enable the required Defender plans and extensions. Direct MDE onboarding is an alternative for endpoint protection but lacks Arc's Azure management, policy, and extension features.

Official lessonHybrid server connection options

units.title: Connect your AWS accounts
units.bodyHtml:
The AWS connector integrates AWS account inventory and AWS Security Hub findings. It can add secure-score recommendations, regulatory compliance, vulnerability assessment, MDE/EDR, and Arc-based server or Kubernetes capabilities according to enabled plans.

  1. Go to Environment settings and add an AWS environment.
  2. Select accounts or organization scope, regions, and scan interval (4, 6, 12, or 24 hours).
  3. Choose Defender plans and automatic provisioning options.
  4. Deploy the generated CloudFormation stack and grant required permissions.
  5. Verify connector health and recommendations.
Official lessonAWS connector configuration

units.title: Connect your GCP accounts
units.bodyHtml:
The GCP connector imports project or organization resources and Security Command Center findings, then evaluates recommendations, secure score, and standards such as CIS.

  1. Enter organization or project details and select plans.
  2. Run the generated gcloud script to create workload identity, service accounts, and policy bindings.
  3. Enable required APIs: IAM, STS, Cloud Resource Manager, IAM Credentials, and Compute.
  4. Create and verify the connector; recommendations can take up to six hours to appear.
Official lessonGCP connector and permissions

units.title: Module assessment
units.bodyHtml:
Review Arc versus direct MDE onboarding, AWS Security Hub versus GCP Security Command Center integration, generated deployment scripts, scan intervals, and required cloud permissions.

Official assessmentMulticloud connection knowledge check

units.title: Summary and resources
units.bodyHtml:
Choose a connection model that supplies enough inventory, identity, telemetry, and control-plane access for the protections you intend to use, while granting least-privileged cloud permissions.

Official moduleSummary and resources

modules.title: Manage your cloud security posture management
modules.focus: Focus: secure score, recommendations, regulatory compliance, Azure Policy, and security workbooks.

units.title: Introduction
units.bodyHtml:
Cloud security posture management continuously identifies configuration risk and turns it into prioritized, measurable improvement. The central views are secure score, recommendations, compliance, and workbooks.

Official moduleCSPM objectives

units.title: Explore Secure Score
units.bodyHtml:
Secure score summarizes how well an environment satisfies Defender for Cloud recommendations. Recommendations are grouped into security controls. A control awards its available points only when every applicable recommendation for a resource is satisfied.

Use score impact, affected resources, threat context, and remediation effort together when prioritizing. A higher score means lower identified configuration risk, but it is not proof that the environment is breach-free.

Official lessonScoring model and controls

units.title: Explore Recommendations
units.bodyHtml:
Recommendations evaluate resources against the Microsoft Cloud Security Benchmark and assigned policy or compliance initiatives. Findings can be healthy, unhealthy, or not applicable. Related recommendations can be prerequisites, alternatives, or dependencies.

Open a recommendation to understand rationale, affected resources, remediation steps, and exemptions. Where available, Fix supports bulk remediation after you review the impact.

Exam takeaway: recommendations are actionable assessments; secure score is their aggregated measurement.
Official lessonRecommendations and remediation

units.title: Measure and enforce regulatory compliance
units.bodyHtml:
Compliance standards are represented as Azure Policy initiatives assigned to a management group or subscription. The dashboard maps resource assessments to controls and shows passing and failing requirements. The Microsoft Cloud Security Benchmark is the default standard; additional standards require appropriate enhanced capabilities.

  1. Assign the initiative at the correct scope.
  2. Wait for policy evaluation.
  3. Review failing controls and underlying assessments.
  4. Remediate, exempt with governance, and export evidence where needed.
Official lessonStandards and Azure Policy initiatives

units.title: Understand Workbooks
units.bodyHtml:
Azure Monitor workbooks turn security data into interactive reports. Built-in Defender for Cloud examples include Secure Score over Time, System Updates, Vulnerability Assessment Findings, Compliance Over Time, and Active Alerts. Customize or clone a workbook for operational reporting without altering the source data.

Official lessonBuilt-in security workbooks

units.title: Module assessment
units.bodyHtml:
Differentiate secure score, recommendation status, bulk Fix remediation, regulatory initiatives, exemptions, and workbooks. Know which view answers measurement, action, assurance, or reporting questions.

Official assessmentCSPM knowledge check

units.title: Summary and resources
units.bodyHtml:
Operate CSPM as a cycle: measure posture, prioritize recommendations, remediate or govern exceptions, validate compliance, and track improvement over time.

Official moduleSummary and resources

modules.title: Explain cloud workload protections in Microsoft Defender for Cloud
modules.focus: Focus: workload-specific protection plans, telemetry sources, detection coverage, and investigation context.

units.title: Introduction
units.bodyHtml:
Cloud workload protection applies threat detection tuned to the service being protected. Each Defender plan uses workload-native signals, Microsoft threat intelligence, and behavioral analytics to detect different attack paths.

Official moduleWorkload protection objectives

units.title: Understand Microsoft Defender for Servers
units.bodyHtml:
PlanCore coverage
Plan 1MDE automatic onboarding, endpoint detection and response, security alerts, and vulnerability assessment.
Plan 2Plan 1 plus agentless discovery, vulnerability and malware scanning, secrets discovery, security baselines, OS-update assessment, file integrity monitoring, just-in-time VM access, network map, and additional detections.

Coverage can extend to Azure VMs, Azure Arc-enabled servers, and supported multicloud machines.

Official lessonDefender for Servers plans

units.title: Understand Microsoft Defender for App Service
units.bodyHtml:
Defender for App Service provides Azure-native, agentless protection for supported App Service workloads. It analyzes platform and application signals, inbound requests, and management operations to detect web attacks, suspicious access, and post-exploitation behavior.

Official lessonApp Service runtime protection

units.title: Understand Microsoft Defender for Storage
units.bodyHtml:
Defender for Storage protects Azure Blob Storage, Azure Files, and Data Lake Storage against suspicious access, data exfiltration, permission changes, and malicious uploads. Malware reputation based on file hashes is not the same as inspecting file contents; configure current malware-scanning capabilities when content scanning is required.

Official lessonStorage threat detection

units.title: Understand Microsoft Defender for SQL
units.bodyHtml:
Defender for SQL combines vulnerability assessment with advanced threat protection for Azure SQL Database, Managed Instance, Synapse SQL, and SQL servers on machines. Detections include SQL injection, anomalous access, brute force, privilege abuse, and suspicious query behavior.

Official lessonSQL assessment and threat protection

units.title: Understand Microsoft Defender for open-source databases
units.bodyHtml:
Defender for open-source relational databases monitors supported PostgreSQL, MySQL, and related database services for unusual access, anomalous queries, brute-force attempts, suspicious applications, and potentially compromised credentials.

Official lessonOpen-source database detections

units.title: Understand Microsoft Defender for Key Vault
units.bodyHtml:
Defender for Key Vault detects unusual or potentially harmful attempts to access keys, secrets, and certificates. Alerts include context such as user principal, source IP, operation, and object ID. A familiar identity or application does not automatically make anomalous access benign.

Official lessonKey Vault anomaly detection

units.title: Understand Microsoft Defender for Resource Manager
units.bodyHtml:
Defender for Resource Manager analyzes Azure control-plane operations made through the portal, API, CLI, or automation. It can identify suspicious source IPs, antimalware disabling, malicious VM extensions, tools such as PowerZure or MicroBurst, and lateral movement from control plane to data plane.

Investigate the alert together with the Azure Activity Log, identity sign-in data, affected resource configuration, and any subsequent data-plane actions.

Official lessonControl-plane threat detection

units.title: Understand Microsoft Defender for DNS
units.bodyHtml:
DNS telemetry reveals attempted communication even when payload traffic is encrypted. Defender for DNS identifies tunneling, command-and-control infrastructure, malicious or phishing domains, cryptomining destinations, and suspicious resolvers.

Official lessonDNS-based detections

units.title: Understand Microsoft Defender for Containers
units.bodyHtml:
Defender for Containers covers supported AKS, EKS, and Arc-enabled Kubernetes environments. It combines cluster hardening recommendations, image vulnerability assessment, Kubernetes policy, node and control-plane signals, audit logs, and runtime detections.

  • Find vulnerable images in registries and identify where they are running.
  • Assess cluster configuration and admission-policy compliance.
  • Detect suspicious commands, privilege escalation, exposed dashboards, and anomalous API activity.
Official lessonContainer posture and runtime protection

units.title: Understand Microsoft Defender additional protections
units.bodyHtml:
Additional coverage can include network-layer analytics based on flow data, Defender for Cosmos DB, Web Application Firewall alerts, DDoS Protection alerts, and Defender for Cloud Apps integration. These signals enrich investigations across identity, application, network, and data layers.

Official lessonNetwork, data, WAF, and DDoS protections

units.title: Module assessment
units.bodyHtml:
Match each workload to its telemetry and detections: server endpoint signals, App Service platform logs, storage operations, database queries, Key Vault object access, ARM control-plane operations, DNS queries, or Kubernetes audit/runtime data.

Official assessmentWorkload protection knowledge check

units.title: Summary and resources
units.bodyHtml:
Select workload plans according to deployed services, risk, and required detection depth. Investigate alerts with the service-native logs and entity context appropriate to that workload.

Official moduleSummary and resources

modules.title: Remediate security alerts using Microsoft Defender for Cloud
modules.focus: Focus: alert triage, incidents, response automation, suppression rules, threat intelligence, and resource-specific response.

units.title: Introduction
units.bodyHtml:
Defender for Cloud turns workload telemetry into prioritized alerts and correlated incidents. Effective response validates the signal, contains the threat, remediates affected resources, and reduces recurrence.

Official moduleAlert response objectives

units.title: Understand security alerts
units.bodyHtml:
Detections use global threat intelligence, behavioral analytics, and anomaly detection. Alerts have high, medium, low, or informational severity and can map to MITRE ATT&CK tactics. Cloud Smart Alert Correlation groups related alerts into an incident, representing a broader attack story.

The alert page provides affected resources, entities such as IPs, files, users, and processes, investigation context, and a Take action area.

Exam takeaway: an alert is one detection; an incident is a correlated set of alerts and entities.
Official lessonAlerts, incidents, severity, and entities

units.title: Remediate alerts and automate responses
units.bodyHtml:
Use Take action to mitigate the current threat, prevent similar attacks, trigger a Logic App, or create a suppression rule. Workflow automation can invoke Logic Apps automatically when alerts or recommendations match configured conditions such as subscription, resource type, alert name, or severity.

  1. Validate and scope the alert.
  2. Contain affected identities, resources, or network paths.
  3. Remove persistence and remediate configuration weaknesses.
  4. Restore safely and monitor for recurrence.
  5. Automate repeatable, well-tested actions.
Official lessonResponse actions and Logic Apps

units.title: Suppress alerts from Defender for Cloud
units.bodyHtml:
Use suppression only for a validated false-positive or known benign pattern. Rules can match attributes such as IP, process, user, resource, or location and can expire after a defined period, up to six months in the lesson. Simulate the rule before activation.

The underlying alert is still generated but is automatically dismissed. Keep scope narrow, document ownership and rationale, and review rules before expiration.

Official lessonSuppression-rule scope and lifecycle

units.title: Generate threat intelligence reports
units.bodyHtml:
Threat intelligence reports provide context beyond one alert. Report types include Activity Group, Campaign, and Threat Summary. They can describe the actor, objectives, campaigns, tactics, techniques and procedures, indicators of compromise, victimology, and recommended defenses.

Official lessonThreat intelligence report types

units.title: Respond to alerts from Azure resources
units.bodyHtml:
Resource-specific response combines alert context with native logs. For a Key Vault alert, validate the user or service principal, source IP, operation, and targeted object; revoke or rotate exposed credentials; restrict network access; inspect Activity and diagnostic logs; and apply the associated security recommendations.

Use the same pattern for other resources: validate actor and action, contain identity/network/resource, preserve evidence, remediate the exploited weakness, and monitor.

Official lessonResource-specific investigation and containment

units.title: Module assessment
units.bodyHtml:
Review alert versus incident, severity and MITRE mapping, Take action options, workflow automation, suppression simulation and expiration, threat-intelligence reports, and resource-native investigation.

Official assessmentAlert remediation knowledge check

units.title: Summary and resources
units.bodyHtml:
Triage with context, correlate the full incident, contain quickly, remediate root causes, automate deterministic actions, and suppress only well-understood benign patterns.

Official moduleSummary and resources
